<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] 14 E2E test files with no shared fixtures module detected; if setup/teardown logic is duplicated across files, a shared helper could reduce maintenance burden.
[observation] [info] Perfect cohesion and zero coupling are ideal for an E2E suite — these tests correctly treat the CLI as a black box.
[observation] [info] architecture-policy.test.js should be reviewed periodically to ensure it encodes current tier boundaries; policy tests that are not updated alongside architectural changes become stale and misleading.
[pattern] [warning] E2E suite has zero source-level coupling but a hidden build-time dependency on all packages; CI must enforce a build step before e2e execution. This should be documented or enforced via a pre-test script to prevent silent false-negatives when a package fails to compile.
[anti-pattern] [warning] MCP HTTP transport (the recommended integration path) has no E2E test coverage; the suite tests CLI process boundaries but not the HTTP session lifecycle, leaving the primary MCP surface unvalidated at the process boundary level.
[anti-pattern] [warning] No shared E2E fixture or helper module detected across 14 test files; duplicated process-spawn and environment setup logic increases maintenance burden and risks inconsistent test environments between files — extract common setup into a shared e2e-helpers module.
[anti-pattern] [warning] architecture-policy.test.js encodes zone IDs and tier boundaries statically; zone renames or structural changes will not automatically invalidate the policy assertions, creating a category of silent false-passes — tie policy checks to the live zone graph output rather than hardcoded identifiers.
[suggestion] [info] Document why e2e tests use .js rather than .ts (e.g. in a README or comment header in the test directory) — without documentation this reads as an oversight; with a one-line comment ('plain JS avoids a compile step since these tests spawn compiled dist/ binaries') it becomes an explicit convention.
[suggestion] [info] Rename architecture-policy.test.js to cli-architecture-policy.test.js (or place it in a policy/ subdirectory) to align with the cli-*.test.js naming convention followed by the other 13 files — inconsistent naming makes the file harder to discover in directory listings and CI output.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm these tests operate at the process boundary — spawning real CLI processes rather than importing production modules, which provides maximum integration confidence.
- Test coverage spans CLI orchestration, per-command delegation, architecture policy enforcement, and web server lifecycle, indicating good breadth across the monorepo's public surface.
- Zero coupling is appropriate here, but verify that architecture-policy.test.js actively enforces import-graph rules (e.g., no cross-tier violations) so it remains a living constraint rather than documentation.
- Perfect cohesion and zero coupling are ideal for an E2E suite — these tests correctly treat the CLI as a black box.
- 14 E2E test files with no shared fixtures module detected; if setup/teardown logic is duplicated across files, a shared helper could reduce maintenance burden.
- architecture-policy.test.js should be reviewed periodically to ensure it encodes current tier boundaries; policy tests that are not updated alongside architectural changes become stale and misleading.
- E2E tests spawn real CLI processes against built dist/ artifacts, creating an implicit build-time dependency on every package in the monorepo — this dependency is invisible to the zone coupling metric (which measures source imports only) but is a real constraint: all packages must be built before the E2E suite can run, meaning any build failure in any zone will silently block E2E coverage.
- E2E suite has zero source-level coupling but a hidden build-time dependency on all packages; CI must enforce a build step before e2e execution. This should be documented or enforced via a pre-test script to prevent silent false-negatives when a package fails to compile.
- The 14 E2E test files each bootstrap their own test environment with no detected shared fixture module; as CLI surface area grows, duplicated setup/teardown logic across files becomes a maintenance liability and a source of subtle environment inconsistencies between test files.
- The architecture-policy test encodes the import-graph rules at a point in time but has no mechanism to auto-update when zone IDs change (e.g., zone renames or merges); stale policy assertions will pass silently rather than failing loudly, giving false confidence that architectural rules are being enforced.
- E2E suite validates CLI commands but has no coverage of the MCP HTTP transport session lifecycle (session creation, Mcp-Session-Id header propagation, session teardown) — as HTTP MCP becomes the recommended transport, this gap leaves the primary integration surface without process-boundary test coverage.
- No shared E2E fixture or helper module detected across 14 test files; duplicated process-spawn and environment setup logic increases maintenance burden and risks inconsistent test environments between files — extract common setup into a shared e2e-helpers module.
- architecture-policy.test.js encodes zone IDs and tier boundaries statically; zone renames or structural changes will not automatically invalidate the policy assertions, creating a category of silent false-passes — tie policy checks to the live zone graph output rather than hardcoded identifiers.
- MCP HTTP transport (the recommended integration path) has no E2E test coverage; the suite tests CLI process boundaries but not the HTTP session lifecycle, leaving the primary MCP surface unvalidated at the process boundary level.
- All 14 E2E test files use the .js extension while the rest of the monorepo is TypeScript-first — this is likely intentional (avoiding a build step for tests that spawn CLI processes) but is undocumented, making it appear as an oversight rather than a deliberate choice.
- 12 of the 14 files follow a 'cli-*.test.js' naming convention but 'architecture-policy.test.js' breaks the pattern — it neither has a 'cli-' prefix nor a subject-verb suffix, making it harder to discover alongside the command-specific tests it conceptually accompanies.
- Document why e2e tests use .js rather than .ts (e.g. in a README or comment header in the test directory) — without documentation this reads as an oversight; with a one-line comment ('plain JS avoids a compile step since these tests spawn compiled dist/ binaries') it becomes an explicit convention.
- Rename architecture-policy.test.js to cli-architecture-policy.test.js (or place it in a policy/ subdirectory) to align with the cli-*.test.js naming convention followed by the other 13 files — inconsistent naming makes the file harder to discover in directory listings and CI output.
- [call graph] 367 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
